title: Getting Started with because
text: R package designed easily perform causal inference Bayesian Structural Equation Models JAGS. package integrates methods proposed von Hardenberg & Gonzalez-Voyer (2025) fit Phylogenetic Bayesian Structural Equation Models (PhyBaSE) extends types covariance structures (eg. spatial autocorrelation, genetic relatedness etc.). main features: Causal Inference d-Separation: Testing conditional independencies implied causal model. Model Diagnostics Comparison: Checking convergence, posterior predictive checks, comparing models WAIC LOO-CV. Mediation Analysis: Decomposing effects direct indirect components. Gaussian (continuous data) Binomial (binary/proportion data) Multinomial (unordered categorical data) Ordinal (ordered categorical data) Poisson (count data) Negative Binomial (overdispersed count data) Zero-inflated Poisson (ZIP) Negative Binomial (ZINB) Deterministic Nodes: Interactions, thresholds mathematical transformations. Custom Priors Mechanistic Constraints: Incorporating prior knowledge mechanistic constraints. Bayesian Missing Data Imputation: Handling missing data simultaneous Bayesian imputation within SEM. Latent Variables MAG Approach: Causal inference presence latent variables using MAG approach Shipley & Douda (2021). Creating Custom Distribution Families: Extending user-defined JAGS distributions. Advanced Model Specifications: random effects (lme4-style (1|group) notation, mixed models, nested designs), polynomial terms, categorical predictors, measurement error. Multiscale & Hierarchical Data: Data different resolutions (e.g. climate physiology) correct degrees freedom d-separation tests. Phylogenetic Path Analysis: Using Phylogenetic Bayesian Structural Equation Model approach (PhyBaSE, von Hardenberg & Gonzalez-Voyer, 2025) .phybase extension.

code: # set seed for reproducibility set.seed(67)  # Simulate predictor n <- 100 X <- rnorm(n = n, mean = 50, sd = 10)  # Generate response with the chosen intercept (alpha) and slope (beta) alpha <- 20 beta <- 0.5 Y <- alpha + beta * X + rnorm(n, mean = 0, sd = 10)  # Combine into data frame sim.dat <- data.frame(X, Y)  # Fit linear model with lm() function for comparison summary(lm(Y ~ X, data = sim.dat)) # Define the equations equations <- list(Y ~ X)  # Fit the model fit <- because(   equations = equations,   data = sim.dat )  # View results summary(fit) # Plot trace plots plot(fit$samples) # Generate JAGS model code jags_model_code <- because_model(   equations = equations )  cat(jags_model_code$model)

title: Causal inference with d-separation
text: simple linear regression presented example previous vignette, modeled relationship two variables, X Y. However, regression tells us correlations, causation. can say X related Y also X predicts Y, say X causes Y. indeed three reasons X Y correlate (real data course, data simulated predefined correlation!): X → Y: X causes Y Y → X: Y causes X X ← Z → Y: common cause Z causing . problem correlation alone distinguish three scenarios. Let’s look classic example correlative study trying establish storks deliver babies (Matthews, 2000). investigated example previously Gonzalez-Voyer & von Hardenberg (2014), data tutorial analysing example traditional path analysis available supplementary materials book chapter : https://github.com/achazhardenberg/mpcm-OPM. Let’s load storks dataframe available package take look relationship number stork pairs number babies born different European countries (thousands per year):  can see appears positive correlation number stork pairs number babies born can confirm simple linear regression: positive relationship stork pairs births confirmed (p = 0.008, already reported Matthews, 2000), mean storks deliver babies? influenced third variable, Country Area? Let’s check Area correlates Births Storks: Indeed, Births Storks correlate significantly Area (p < 0.0001 p = 0.015 respectively). Matthews (2000) argued observed correlation Storks Births actually due confounding effect Area: larger countries storks births, leading spurious correlation Storks Births. Indeed, controlling statistically Area multiple regression, effect Storks Births becomes non-significant: causal inference framework, can represent three variables causal relationships using Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG):  Directed Acyclic Graphs (DAGs) explicitly state causal assumptions, multiple regression effectively tested conditional independence implied DAG: Storks || Birth | {Area} (Storks Births independent conditional Area) translates statistically d-separation test just performed including Area covariate regression Births Storks. causal model therefore consistent data. “Consistent” means conditional independencies implied DAG hold data, prove causal model actually correct. models may also consistent data three variables impossible distinguish different causal models. direct reader Gonzalez-Voyer & von Hardenberg (2014) detailed discussion example Shipley (2000, 2003, 2016) theoretical foundations d-separation. Gonzalez-Voyer & von Hardenberg (2014) also explored causal relationships three variables fourth variable present dataset: Humans (Human population size millions) proposed causal model tested using classic piecewise Path Analysis (follow online tutorial want replicate analysis).  Let’s now see test causal model . First, specify structural equations implied DAG: Next, good practice standardize variables prior analysis, path coefficients can interpreted standardized effects (also helps convergence mcmc chains): Now can fit model (), enabling d-separation tests dsep = TRUE argument: can see three d-separation tests pass, indicating conditional independencies implied causal model hold data. title: Causal inference with d-separation
text: Test: conditional independence tested: Birth _||_ Storks | {Area} means “Storks independent Birth given Area” - _||_: Independence symbol - | {}: Conditioning set (controlling ) Param Estimate: beta estimate variable tested conditional independence - Birth _||_ Storks | {Area}, tests beta_Birth_Storks model Birth ~ Storks + Area - independent, beta ~0 LowerCI, UpperCI: Posterior mean 95% credible interval beta parameter tested - independent, Credibility intervals include zero. Rhat, n.eff: MCMC diagnostics beta parameter tested - Rhat ~ 1 indicates convergence - n.eff: Effective sample size can now fit model without d-separation tests obtain parameter estimates: parameters converged (Rhat ~ 1) path coefficients positive, indicating larger Area leads Storks Births, Births lead larger Human populations, expected. title: Causal inference with d-separation
text: summary output , parameter names follow specific notation indicate relationships modelled. example, beta_Birth_Area represents path coefficient (beta) effect Area Births. format beta_<DependentVariable>_<IndependentVariable>. notation helps quickly identify variables involved path model. alpha parameters represent intercepts equation, sigma parameters represent residual standard deviations dependent variable. , example, path going Area Births represented linear regression: Birth ~ alphaBirth + beta_Birth_Area * Area + error, error normally distributed standard deviation = sigmaBirth.

title: Model Diagnostics and Comparison
text: fitting Bayesian SEM , analysis end parameter estimation. responsible workflow requires: Convergence diagnostics: MCMC chains mix well? Posterior predictive checks: model reproduce observed data patterns? Model comparison: competing causal structure best supported data? vignette walks diagnostic comparison tools available .

title: Model Diagnostics and Comparison
text: use three-variable causal chain running example throughout.
code: library(because)  set.seed(42) N <- 150  Temperature <- rnorm(N, 0, 1)    # Exogenous NDVI        <- 0.6 * Temperature + rnorm(N, 0, 0.8)   # Mediator Abundance   <- 0.4 * NDVI + 0.3 * Temperature + rnorm(N, 0, 1)  sim_data <- data.frame(Temperature, NDVI, Abundance) # Causal model: Temperature -> NDVI -> Abundance, Temperature -> Abundance fit <- because(   equations = list(     NDVI      ~ Temperature,     Abundance ~ NDVI + Temperature   ),   data    = sim_data,   dsep    = TRUE,   WAIC    = TRUE,   # Required for model comparison   n.iter  = 12500,   n.burnin = 2500 )  summary(fit)

title: Model Diagnostics and Comparison
text: summary() output includes Rhat (Gelman-Rubin convergence diagnostic) n.eff (effective sample size) parameter. Rhat < 1.05: Excellent convergence. Rhat 1.05–1.1: Acceptable parameters; consider running iterations. Rhat > 1.1: Non-convergence. chains mixed. interpret estimates. n.eff > 100: Generally sufficient reliable posterior summaries. n.eff < 100: Estimates may unstable. Increase n.iter n.thin.
code: # The stored summary object has $statistics containing Rhat and n.eff sum_stats <- fit$summary$statistics  # Identify parameters with potential convergence issues if (!is.null(sum_stats) && "Rhat" %in% colnames(sum_stats)) {   high_rhat <- sum_stats[sum_stats[, "Rhat"] > 1.05, , drop = FALSE]   if (nrow(high_rhat) == 0) {     cat("All parameters converged (Rhat < 1.05).\n")   } else {     print(high_rhat)   } } else {   # summary() prints results to console; inspect Rhat column visually   summary(fit) }

title: Model Diagnostics and Comparison
text: Trace plots show MCMC samples time chain. well-mixed chain looks like “fuzzy caterpillar”: dense, stationary, without long-term trends. look : - Multiple chains (coloured differently) overlapping completely. - visible trend, drift, “stickiness” (chains staying value many consecutive iterations).
code: # Plot traces for all monitored parameters plot(fit$samples) # Focus on a specific parameter library(coda) plot(fit$samples[, "beta_Abundance_NDVI"])

title: Model Diagnostics and Comparison
text: High autocorrelation within chains reduces effective sample size. autocorrelation remains high large lags, increase n.thin thin chains aggressively.
code: autocorr.plot(fit$samples[, "beta_Abundance_NDVI"])

title: Model Diagnostics and Comparison
text: Posterior predictive checks (PPCs) compare observed data data simulated fitted model. model good fit, simulated data resemble real data. implements PPCs via pp_check(), wrapper around bayesplot package.

title: Model Diagnostics and Comparison
text: thick line density observed data; thin lines densities 50 posterior predictive draws. overlap well. Systematic mismatches (e.g., model consistently underestimates extreme values) indicate model misspecification.
code: # Overlay posterior predictive densities on the observed density pp_check(fit, resp = "Abundance", type = "dens_overlay", ndraws = 50)

title: Model Diagnostics and Comparison
text: histogram shows distribution test statistic (e.g., mean) across posterior predictive datasets. vertical line marks observed statistic. observed value falls tails predictive distribution, model may misspecified aspect data.
code: # Compare the observed mean and SD to the posterior predictive distribution pp_check(fit, resp = "Abundance", type = "stat", stat = "mean") pp_check(fit, resp = "Abundance", type = "stat", stat = "sd")

Enable fit time WAIC = TRUE: WAIC output contains: WAIC: information criterion value (lower = better). lppd: Log pointwise predictive density (higher = better fit). p_waic: Effective number parameters (complexity penalty).

title: Model Diagnostics and Comparison
text: output table ranks models WAIC. deltaWAIC column gives difference best model; weight approximate model weight (higher = support).
code: # Full model: Temperature -> NDVI -> Abundance, Temperature -> Abundance fit_full <- because(   equations = list(     NDVI      ~ Temperature,     Abundance ~ NDVI + Temperature   ),   data = sim_data, WAIC = TRUE, quiet = TRUE )  # Simpler model: Temperature only acts through NDVI (no direct path) fit_mediated <- because(   equations = list(     NDVI      ~ Temperature,     Abundance ~ NDVI   ),   data = sim_data, WAIC = TRUE, quiet = TRUE )  # Compare using WAIC because_compare(fit_full, fit_mediated)

what: 5.2 Run and Compare Multiple Specifications Simultaneously
title: Model Diagnostics and Comparison
text: larger model selection exercise, because_compare() can run multiple model specifications parallel return comparison table directly:
code: model_specs <- list(   full_mediation = list(     equations = list(NDVI ~ Temperature, Abundance ~ NDVI + Temperature)   ),   partial_mediation = list(     equations = list(NDVI ~ Temperature, Abundance ~ NDVI)   ),   direct_only = list(     equations = list(Abundance ~ Temperature)   ) )  comparison_result <- because_compare(   model_specs = model_specs,   data        = sim_data,   n.cores     = 2,        # Run models in parallel   WAIC        = TRUE,   quiet       = TRUE )  print(comparison_result$comparison)

title: Model Diagnostics and Comparison
text: rigorous comparison, use LOO-CV: elpd_diff column shows difference expected log predictive density (positive = better). standard error difference (se_diff) considered: |elpd_diff| < 2 * se_diff, models approximately equivalent predictive performance.
code: loo1 <- because_loo(fit_full) loo2 <- because_loo(fit_mediated)  # loo_compare from the loo package library(loo) loo_compare(loo1, loo2)

title: Model Diagnostics and Comparison
text: comparing many alternative DAGs share conditional independence claims, can reuse already-computed d-separation tests via reuse_models argument, avoiding redundant JAGS runs:
code: fit_base <- because(   equations = list(NDVI ~ Temperature, Abundance ~ NDVI + Temperature),   data = sim_data, dsep = TRUE, quiet = TRUE )  # Fit a variant that reuses any matching tests from fit_base fit_variant <- because(   equations = list(NDVI ~ Temperature, Abundance ~ NDVI),   data        = sim_data,   dsep        = TRUE,   reuse_models = list(fit_base),  # Scan for reusable tests   quiet       = TRUE )

WAIC/LOO comparison supports chosen causal structure plausible alternatives.

title: Mediation Analysis
text: Causal inference often requires investigating effect occurs. XX affect YY directly, work mediator MM? package provides fully automated Bayesian mediation analysis tool, because_mediation(), decomposes Total Effect exposure outcome : 1. Direct Effect: effect X→YX \Y mediated variables graph. 2. Indirect Effect(s): effect propagated intermediate variables (X→M→YX \M \Y). calculated multiplying posterior distributions coefficients along path, preserving full uncertainty quantification.

title: Mediation Analysis
text: example, investigate Elevation affects Plant Abundance. hypothesize Elevation acts causal chain involving Temperature Soil Moisture: Elevation determines Temperature (higher elevation →\lower temperature). Temperature influences Soil Moisture (lower temperature →\lower evaporation →\higher moisture). Abundance driven Moisture, Temperature, potentially direct effect Elevation (e.g., due UV radiation partial pressure gases).

title: Mediation Analysis
text: simulate N=200N=200 plots along elevation gradient.
code: library(because)  set.seed(42) N <- 200  # 1. Elevation (Exogenous variable) # Ranges roughly from 500m to 1500m Elevation <- rnorm(N, mean = 1000, sd = 200)  # 2. Temperature (Mediator 1) # Decreases with Elevation (Lapse rate approx effect) # Coef: -0.01 implies 100m climb -> -1 degree C Temp <- 25 - 0.01 * Elevation + rnorm(N, sd = 2)  # 3. Moisture (Mediator 2) # Driven by Temperature. Cooler -> Moister. # We model a negative relationship with Temp. Moisture <- 20 - 2 * Temp + rnorm(N, sd = 5)  # 4. Plant Abundance (Outcome) # - Positive effect of Moisture (+0.5) # - Positive effect of Temperature (+1.5) # - Direct negative effect of Elevation (-0.005) due to harsh conditions Abundance <- 10 + 0.5 * Moisture + 1.5 * Temp - 0.005 * Elevation + rnorm(N, sd = 10)  eco_data <- data.frame(Elevation, Temp, Moisture, Abundance) head(eco_data)

title: Mediation Analysis
text: Important: mediation analysis, highly recommended standardize continuous variables (mean = 0, sd = 1) fitting. Standardization ensures : 1. Coefficients comparable: effects expressed standard deviation units (standardized effects). 2. Scale Invariance: calculation Indirect Effects (product coefficients) interpretable relative Total Effect. 3. Convergence: MCMC sampling often behaves better standardized scales.
code: # Standardize all variables eco_data_std <- scale(eco_data) head(eco_data_std)

title: Mediation Analysis
text: define structural equations reflecting causal DAG. Notice chain: Elevation -> Temp -> Moisture -> Abundance. can also plot fitted causal model standardised paths:
code: # Define the structural equations eco_eqs <- list(   Temp ~ Elevation,   Moisture ~ Temp,   Abundance ~ Moisture + Temp + Elevation )  # Fit the model # We use a short chain for demonstration purposes. Use more iterations for real analysis. fit <- because(   equations = eco_eqs,   data = eco_data_std,   n.iter = 2000 ) summary(fit) plot_dag(fit)

title: Mediation Analysis
text: want understand Total Effect Elevation Abundance, decompose direct indirect components.
code: # Run Mediation Analysis for Elevation -> Abundance med_results <- because_mediation(fit, exposure = "Elevation", outcome = "Abundance")

Elevation lowers Temp (negative). Lower Temp raises Moisture (negative relationship →\positive change moisture). Higher Moisture raises Abundance (positive). chain involves two negative links one positive link, resulting Positive indirect effect. function handles decomposition automatically, providing credibility intervals specific mechanism.

title: Mediation Analysis
text: function calculates indirect effect product coefficients along path. example, long chain: Indirectchain=βElev→Temp×βTemp→Moist×βMoist→Abund \text{Indirect}_{\text{chain}} = \beta_{Elev \Temp} \times \beta_{Temp \Moist} \times \beta_{Moist \Abund}  approach standard linear models. non-linear models, represents approximation average causal effect.

title: Non-Gaussian Distribution Families
text: ecology evolutionary biology, response variables rarely normally distributed. Count data, binary outcomes, proportions, ordered categories require appropriate distributional assumptions. natively supports wide range response families, specified family argument (). vignette walks supported family worked ecological example, demonstrates use marginal_effects() convert link-scale coefficients (log, logit) interpretable expected-value effects.

title: Non-Gaussian Distribution Families
text: family argument accepts named character vector, names response variables values distribution names: Gaussian default, need specify family non-Gaussian responses.
code: library(because)  # Minimal example data set.seed(1) N <- 100 example_data <- data.frame(   Y     = rpois(N, lambda = 3),   Count = rpois(N, lambda = 5),   Presence = rbinom(N, size = 1, prob = 0.4),   X = rnorm(N) )  # Single non-Gaussian response fit <- because(   equations = list(Y ~ X),   data = example_data,   family = c(Y = "poisson"),   quiet = TRUE )  # Multiple responses with different families fit_mixed <- because(   equations = list(     Count ~ X,     Presence ~ X   ),   data = example_data,   family = c(Count = "poisson", Presence = "bernoulli"),   quiet = TRUE )

title: Non-Gaussian Distribution Families
text: Use case: Number offspring, species counts, nest failures, disease incidence. Link function: log — log(mu) = alpha + beta * X summary output gives alpha_Offspring (log-scale intercept) beta_Offspring_* (log-scale slopes). slope 0.5 log scale means one-unit increase predictor multiplies expected count e0.5≈1.65e^{0.5} \approx 1.65.
code: set.seed(42) N <- 150  # Predictors Body_Mass <- rnorm(N, mean = 50, sd = 10)  # kg Habitat_Quality <- runif(N, 0, 1)          # 0-1 index  # True log-linear relationship log_lambda <- 0.5 + 0.03 * Body_Mass + 1.2 * Habitat_Quality Offspring <- rpois(N, lambda = exp(log_lambda))  count_data <- data.frame(   Offspring = Offspring,   Body_Mass = scale(Body_Mass)[, 1],   Habitat_Quality = scale(Habitat_Quality)[, 1] ) # Fit Poisson SEM fit_poisson <- because(   equations = list(Offspring ~ Body_Mass + Habitat_Quality),   data = count_data,   family = c(Offspring = "poisson"),   dsep = FALSE )  summary(fit_poisson)

title: Non-Gaussian Distribution Families
text: Use case: Species presence/absence, survival, reproductive success (yes/). Link function: logit — logit(p) = alpha + beta * X Use "bernoulli" 0/1 binary data "binomial" proportion data known totals.
code: set.seed(42) N <- 200  # Predictors Temperature <- rnorm(N, mean = 15, sd = 5) Precipitation <- rnorm(N, mean = 800, sd = 200)  # True logistic relationship logit_p <- -1 + 0.15 * scale(Temperature) + 0.08 * scale(Precipitation) Presence <- rbinom(N, size = 1, prob = 1 / (1 + exp(-logit_p)))  presence_data <- data.frame(   Presence = Presence,   Temperature = scale(Temperature)[, 1],   Precipitation = scale(Precipitation)[, 1] ) fit_bern <- because(   equations = list(Presence ~ Temperature + Precipitation),   data = presence_data,   family = c(Presence = "bernoulli") )  summary(fit_bern) # Marginal effects: expected change in *probability* of presence per 1-SD increase me_bern <- marginal_effects(fit_bern) print(me_bern)

title: Non-Gaussian Distribution Families
text: Use case: Count data variance greatly exceeds mean (e.g., parasite loads, number plant seeds, abundance data). Poisson model assumes Var(Y)=E(Y)\text{Var}(Y) = E(Y). Ecological count data often show overdispersion (Var(Y)>E(Y)\text{Var}(Y) > E(Y)), can inflate Type error Poisson models. Negative Binomial adds dispersion parameter rr (estimated data) accommodate extra variance. Negative Binomial model substantially lower WAIC data overdispersed. r_Parasite_Load parameter summary captures estimated overdispersion.
code: set.seed(123) N <- 150  Resource <- rnorm(N, 0, 1)  # Overdispersed counts: large variance relative to mean # MASS::rnegbin: mu = exp(0.8 + 0.6*X), size = 2 (small = more overdispersed) library(MASS) Parasite_Load <- rnegbin(N, mu = exp(0.8 + 0.6 * Resource), theta = 2)  nb_data <- data.frame(   Parasite_Load = Parasite_Load,   Resource = Resource ) # Compare Poisson vs. Negative Binomial fit_pois <- because(   equations = list(Parasite_Load ~ Resource),   data = nb_data,   family = c(Parasite_Load = "poisson"),   WAIC = TRUE,   quiet = TRUE )  fit_nb <- because(   equations = list(Parasite_Load ~ Resource),   data = nb_data,   family = c(Parasite_Load = "negbinomial"),   WAIC = TRUE,   quiet = TRUE )  # Compare WAIC (lower = better fit) because_compare(fit_pois, fit_nb)

what: Example 4: Zero-Inflated Models — ZIP and ZINB
title: Non-Gaussian Distribution Families
text: Use case: Count data zeros expected standard Poisson Negative Binomial distribution. Common ecological surveys (many sites detections, plus genuine count variation occupied sites). Zero-Inflated Poisson (ZIP) models data mixture structural zeros Poisson process. ZINB adds Negative Binomial overdispersion.
code: set.seed(99) N <- 200  Habitat <- rnorm(N, 0, 1)  # ZIP: with probability psi, the count is zero (structural zero); # otherwise it follows a Poisson distribution. psi <- 0.4  # 40% structural zeros lambda <- exp(0.5 + 0.7 * Habitat)  structural_zero <- rbinom(N, 1, psi) Abundance <- ifelse(structural_zero == 1, 0, rpois(N, lambda))  zip_data <- data.frame(   Abundance = Abundance,   Habitat = Habitat ) fit_zip <- because(   equations = list(Abundance ~ Habitat),   data = zip_data,   family = c(Abundance = "zip") )  summary(fit_zip) # psi_Abundance: estimated zero-inflation probability # beta_Abundance_Habitat: effect on the Poisson component (log scale) # Use ZINB when counts are also overdispersed fit_zinb <- because(   equations = list(Abundance ~ Habitat),   data = zip_data,   family = c(Abundance = "zinb") )  summary(fit_zinb)

title: Non-Gaussian Distribution Families
text: Use case: Behaviour scores, habitat quality rankings, dominance rank, pain scales, Likert-scale data. Ordinal models estimate set cutpoints (thresholds) divide latent continuous scale categories. proportional odds assumption means single slope parameter describes effect adjacent-category boundaries.
code: set.seed(7) N <- 200  # Predictor: years of experience Dominance_Rank <- rnorm(N, 0, 1)  # Underlying latent variable latent <- 0 + 1.5 * Dominance_Rank + rnorm(N, 0, 1)  # 4 behaviour categories: Subordinate (1), Low (2), Medium (3), Dominant (4) Behaviour <- cut(latent,   breaks = c(-Inf, -1, 0, 1, Inf),   labels = FALSE )  ord_data <- data.frame(   Behaviour = Behaviour,   Dominance_Rank = Dominance_Rank ) fit_ord <- because(   equations = list(Behaviour ~ Dominance_Rank),   data = ord_data,   family = c(Behaviour = "ordinal") )  summary(fit_ord) # cutpoint_Behaviour[k]: thresholds on the latent scale separating categories k and k+1 # beta_Behaviour_Dominance_Rank: effect on the latent scale (positive = higher categories)

code: fit_ord_expanded <- because(   equations = list(Behaviour ~ Dominance_Rank),   data = ord_data,   family = c(Behaviour = "ordinal"),   expand_ordered = TRUE  # Separate slope per adjacent-category boundary )  summary(fit_ord_expanded)

Recommendations: Standardize predictors fitting: data = .data.frame(scale(mydata)). Use iterations: n.iter = 25000 . Check Rhat: parameters Rhat < 1.1. Use NIMBLE engine better mixing non-Gaussian models: engine = "nimble" (see NIMBLE vignette).

title: Deterministic Nodes: Interactions, tresholds and mathematical transformations
text: Standard R formulas (y ~ * B) handle interactions automatically complete data. However, values B missing, unless use standard procedure eliminating rows missing data (cosequent loss power), imputation introduces trap. follow common workflow “impute first, model”, imputation step usually assumes simple linear relationships ignores interaction. often biases interaction estimate towards zero. , just write formula naturally. model imputes missing values estimates interaction simultaneously, ensuring interaction structure preserved imputed data.
code: N <- 100  # Predictors Rain <- rnorm(N) Temp <- rnorm(N)  # Interaction: Growth depends on Rain * Temp Growth <- 0.5 * (Rain * Temp) + rnorm(N, sd = 0.1)  # remove some random values in Rain and Temp Rain[sample(1:N, 10)] <- NA Temp[sample(1:N, 15)] <- NA   data <- data.frame(Growth = Growth, Rain = Rain, Temp = Temp)  # Note the formula: Growth ~ Rain * Temp fit_int <- because(     equations = list(         Growth ~ Rain * Temp     ),     data = data,     n.iter = 1000,     quiet = TRUE ) # The model creates a deterministic node for the interaction  # Notice the parameter name "beta_Growth_Rain_x_Temp" summary(fit_int)

title: Deterministic Nodes: Interactions, tresholds and mathematical transformations
text: can model “tipping points” categorical definitions. example, marmot (Marmota marmota) “Mature” older 2 years.
code: # Predictors Age <- runif(N, 0, 10)  # Deterministic threshold: 1 if Age > 2, else 0 IsMature <- as.numeric(Age > 2)  # Outcome: Mating success depends on Maturity Mating <- 2 * IsMature + rnorm(N, sd = 0.5)  data_logic <- data.frame(Mating = Mating, Age = Age)  # Use I() to wrap the logic fit_logic <- because(     equations = list(         Mating ~ I(Age > 3)     ),     data = data_logic,     n.iter = 1000,     quiet = TRUE )  # The model recovers the effect of the threshold # Parameter: "beta_Mating_Age_gt_2" where "gt" means "greater than" summary(fit_logic)

title: Deterministic Nodes: Interactions, tresholds and mathematical transformations
text: multiple life stages? * Newborn: Age = 0 * Juvenile: Age 1 * Subadult: Age 2-4 * Adult: Age >= 5 can define using sum logical conditions:
code: # Define 4-level class: 1=Newborn, 2=Juv, 3=Sub, 4=Adult # IMPORTANT: The class must be derived from Age so the model understands the structure. Age <- round(runif(N, 0, 10), 0)  AgeClass <- 1 * (Age == 0) +     2 * (Age > 0 & Age < 2) +     3 * (Age >= 2 & Age < 5) +     4 * (Age >= 5)  # Outcome: Social Rank increases with Life Stage Rank <- 1.5 * AgeClass + rnorm(N, sd = 0.5)  data_multi <- data.frame(Rank = Rank, Age = Age, AgeClass = AgeClass)  # because() recovers the slope (~1.5) and confirms the causal path Age -> AgeClass -> Rank fit_multi <- because(     equations = list(         # 1. Link AgeClass to Rank (Causal: Class -> Rank)         Rank ~ AgeClass     ),     data = data_multi,     n.iter = 1000,     quiet = TRUE )  # Check effect of AgeClass on Rank summary(fit_multi)

what: Scenario: The “Broken Logic” of Standard Imputation
title: Deterministic Nodes: Interactions, tresholds and mathematical transformations
text: Imagine missing Age value. want impute . imputer sees two separate columns: Age (NA) IsMature (NA). guesses Age = 6 based body size. independently guesses IsMature = 0 based hormone levels. Result: marmot 6 years old “Immature”. Impossible! IsMature variable impute; formula. MCMC Step 1, model guesses Age = 1. automatically calculates IsMature = 0. MCMC Step 2, model guesses Age = 6. automatically calculates IsMature = 1. Result: Every single sample posterior distribution logically consistent. “physics” model preserved.

text: One strengths Bayesian modeling ability incorporate prior knowledge analysis. package, generally use “uninformative” weakly informative priors default let data speak . However, many cases might want inject specific information: Mechanistic Knowledge: know parameter (like growth rate) must positive lies within biologically feasible range. Previous Studies: estimates previous meta-analysis experiment want use starting point. Measurement Error: know measurement error instrument want fix constrain residual variance. vignette demonstrates use priors argument () override default priors inject mechanistic constraints.

what: Example 1: Comparing Default and Custom Priors
title: Custom Priors and Mechanistic Constraints
text: example, let’s simulate Absolute Growth Rate grams per day (g/day) hypothetical juvenile birds responding ambient temperature.
code: library(because)  set.seed(42) N <- 30 # Ambient temperature in Celsius (Mean 20C) Temp_Raw <- rnorm(N, mean = 20, sd = 5)  # Center it so '0' represents the mean (20C). # This ensures the intercept represents growth at average conditions, # rather than at 0°C (where the bird would be frozen solid!). Temp_Centered <- Temp_Raw - 20  # True relationship: # Baseline growth at mean temp is 10 g/day. # Every degree of warming above mean adds 0.5 g/day. Growth_g_day <- 0.5 * Temp_Centered + 10 + rnorm(N, sd = 2)  df <- data.frame(Temp_Centered, Growth_g_day)

title: Custom Priors and Mechanistic Constraints
text: Let’s fit standard model first. default, assigns weakly informative Gaussian priors (dnorm(0, 0.01)) intercepts (alpha) slopes (beta). corresponds Standard Deviation 10. anchors broad enough uninformative scientific data preventing numerical instability convergence failure (Rhat>1.1Rhat > 1.1) often seen extremely “flat” priors complex hierarchical models. Gaussian response variables, residual standard deviation gets half-uniform prior: sigma_e ~ dunif(0, 100), precision derived tau_e <- 1 / (sigma_e * sigma_e). scale-invariant works safely whether data standardised original (raw) scale.
code: fit_default <- because(     equations = list(Growth_g_day ~ Temp_Centered),     data = df )  summary(fit_default)

previous_headings: Example 1: Comparing Default and Custom Priors
title: Custom Priors and Mechanistic Constraints
text: Now, suppose strong prior knowledge scaling theory previous literature: 1. Intercept (Alpha): mean temperatures (20°C), metabolic constraints suggest growth around 10 g/day. 2. Slope (Beta): expect positive effect temperature, likely around 0.5 g/day/°C. parameter names usually follow convention: Intercepts: alpha_{Response} (e.g., alpha_Growth_g_day) Slopes: beta_{Response}_{Predictor} (e.g., beta_Growth_g_day_Temp_Centered) Residual SD (Gaussian): sigma_e_{Response} (e.g., sigma_e_Growth_g_day) — override priors Gaussian responses. Residual precision (derived): tau_e_{Response} — set automatically sigma_e; set directly unless need legacy dgamma override. Notice credible intervals custom model tighter centered closer priors, especially data sparse (small N).
code: # Define our custom priors my_priors <- list(     # Strong prior on intercept: Mean 10, Precision 100 (SD = 0.1)     alpha_Growth_g_day = "dnorm(10, 100)",      # Informative prior on slope: Mean 0.5, Precision 400 (SD = 0.05)     beta_Growth_g_day_Temp_Centered = "dnorm(0.5, 400)" )  fit_custom <- because(     equations = list(Growth_g_day ~ Temp_Centered),     data = df,     priors = my_priors )  summary(fit_custom)

code: # We can use the helper function plot_posterior() to compare models. # By passing a list of models, they are overlaid on the same plot. # The 'parameter' argument uses partial matching (regex), so "Growth_g_day" # will match both the intercept (alphaGrowth_g_day) and the slope (beta_Growth_g_day...).  plot_posterior(     model = list("Default" = fit_default, "Custom" = fit_custom),     parameter = "Growth_g_day" )

title: Custom Priors and Mechanistic Constraints
text: many biological contexts, negative effect just unlikely, physically impossible. example, consider relationship Body Mass Metabolic Rate (Kleiber’s Law). larger animal simply consume less energy smaller one (negative slope), else equal. physics life requires costs scale positively mass. However, sample size small measurement error huge, might accidentally estimate negative slope. can prevent enforcing positive prior.
code: # Simulate data following Kleiber's Law: MR = a * Mass^0.75 # Taking logs: log(MR) = log(a) + 0.75 * log(Mass) set.seed(42) Mass <- runif(30, 10, 100) # True scaling exponent is 0.75 # We add enough noise that the estimated slope might be negative by chance Log_Mass <- log(Mass) Log_MR <- 1.5 + 0.75 * Log_Mass + rnorm(30, sd = 2.5)  df_kleiber <- data.frame(Log_Mass, Log_MR)  # Prior: The scaling exponent (slope) must be positive # Physics dictates that metabolic cost increases with mass. positive_prior <- list(     beta_Log_MR_Log_Mass = "dnorm(0, 1) T(0, )" )  # 1. Fit Default Model (Unconstrained) - Log-Log regression fit_default_kleiber <- because(     equations = list(Log_MR ~ Log_Mass),     data = df_kleiber )  # 2. Fit Mechanistic Model (Constrained) fit_mech_kleiber <- because(     equations = list(Log_MR ~ Log_Mass),     data = df_kleiber,     priors = positive_prior )  # Compare Estimates summary(fit_default_kleiber) summary(fit_mech_kleiber)  # Visualize: Unconstrained vs. Truncated plot_posterior(     list(Unconstrained = fit_default_kleiber, Mechanistic = fit_mech_kleiber),     parameter = "beta_Log_MR_Log_Mass",     density_args = list(Mechanistic = list(from = 0)) )

title: Custom Priors and Mechanistic Constraints
text: usually estimate residual standard deviation (sigma_e) data. However, might prior knowledge expected noise level — example, laboratory instrument known precision, measurement protocol documented coefficient variation. [!NOTE] Distinction variability argument: variability argument () designed Measurement Error predictors (Error--Variables) repeated measures per individual. Custom priors sigma_e_*, shown , providing information Residual Variance response variable (includes Process Error unrecognised Observation Error). [!TIP] sigma_e tau_e? Since version >= 0.5, priors Gaussian residual error standard deviation scale (sigma_e ~ dunif(0, 100)). scale-invariant: works correctly whether response variable grams tonnes. precision tau_e derived deterministically tau_e <- 1 / (sigma_e * sigma_e) given prior directly — use sigma_e_* override key. example, field protocol experience tells measurement noise growth measurements typically 1–3 g/day, can constrain prior sigma_e accordingly: [!NOTE] Legacy tau_e_* overrides: previously used priors = list(tau_e_Response = "dgamma(...)"), still works — checks tau_e_* entry priors first uses verbatim, bypassing sigma parameterisation entirely. ensures backward compatibility.
code: # Simulate small, noisy dataset set.seed(42) N_small <- 15 Temp_Small <- rnorm(N_small, 20, 5) # Actual residual SD = 3 (quite noisy) Growth_Small <- 0.5 * Temp_Small + rnorm(N_small, sd = 3) df_small <- data.frame(Growth_g_day = Growth_Small, Temp_Centered = Temp_Small - 20)  # 1. Default Model # dunif(0, 100) on sigma_e: completely open to any residual SD up to 100. # With only 15 observations the posterior will be wide. fit_default_var <- because(     equations = list(Growth_g_day ~ Temp_Centered),     data = df_small,     quiet = TRUE )  # 2. Constrained Model # Prior knowledge: residual SD should be small, around 1 g/day. # dunif(0, 2) restricts sigma_e to [0, 2], strongly informative. variance_prior <- list(     sigma_e_Growth_g_day = "dunif(0, 2)" )  fit_constrained_var <- because(     equations = list(Growth_g_day ~ Temp_Centered),     data = df_small,     priors = variance_prior,     quiet = TRUE )  # Visualize: The constrained posterior for sigma will be shifted left (towards 1) and sharper plot_posterior(     list(Default = fit_default_var, Constrained = fit_constrained_var),     parameter = "sigma" )

title: Multiscale Causal Models
text: Many ecological evolutionary datasets inherently multiscale: processes operate different resolutions, annual climate driving individual-level physiology. settings, variables measured different scales fundamentally different sample sizes thus different statistical power. climate covariate averaged 10 years provides 10 degrees freedom testing effects, regardless many individual observations exist within years. package explicitly handles structure multiscale engine. data supplied named list data frames, one per scale, engine: Correctly maps variable natural scale. Uses appropriate degrees freedom d-separation test: year-scale tests use NyearsN_{years}, individual-scale tests use NindividualsN_{individuals}, cross-scale tests fitted lower scale using whatever random effects researcher specified structural equations. Skips tests trivially satisfied design multiscale structure (e.g., orthogonal branches share observations).

what: Example: Climate, Resources, and Reproductive Success
title: Multiscale Causal Models
text: Consider long-term monitoring study wild mammal population. 10 years, ecologists record: Year-scale variables (1 row per year): mean winter temperature (Temp) annual primary productivity (NDVI). Individual-scale variables (multiple rows per individual per year): body mass (Mass) number offspring (Offspring). causal hypothesis : Winter temperature controls primary productivity (Temp → NDVI). Productivity temperature together drive individual body mass (NDVI → Mass, Temp → Mass). Body mass determines reproductive success (Mass → Offspring). cross-scale causal chain: climate acts year scale, ultimate effect offspring mediated individual body condition.
code: library(because)  dag <- list(   NDVI       ~ Temp,   Mass       ~ NDVI + Temp,   Offspring  ~ Mass )  plot_dag(dag)

title: Multiscale Causal Models
text: use multiscale modeling, data must structured named list data frames, list element corresponds different scale study.

title: Multiscale Causal Models
text: Every variable model (predictors responses) must live exactly one data frame (one corresponding scale measured). Year-scale variables (like Temp) year data frame. Individual-scale variables (like Mass) individual data frame. repeat year-level variables individual-level data frame. automatically handle cross-level mapping using link_vars.

title: Multiscale Causal Models

title: Multiscale Causal Models
code: set.seed(42)  n_years <- 10 n_ind   <- 30   # 10 individuals followed over time  # --- Year-scale data --- year_df <- data.frame(   Year = 1:n_years,   Temp = rnorm(n_years, mean = 0, sd = 1),   NDVI = NA_real_ ) year_df$NDVI <- 0.7 * year_df$Temp + rnorm(n_years, 0, 0.5)  # --- Individual-scale (Repeated Measures) data --- ind_df <- expand.grid(   ID   = 1:n_ind,   Year = 1:n_years )  # For simulation, each individual has a unique "personality" (random effect) # that stays constant across years. ind_personality <- rnorm(n_ind, 0, 0.5) names(ind_personality) <- 1:n_ind  # Look up year-scale and individual-scale components temp_lookup <- year_df$Temp[ind_df$Year] ndvi_lookup <- year_df$NDVI[ind_df$Year] pers_lookup <- ind_personality[as.character(ind_df$ID)]  # Mass depends on year-scale NDVI/Temp AND the individual's personality ind_df$Mass <- (   1.2 * ndvi_lookup +    0.4 * temp_lookup +    pers_lookup +        # <-- Individual-scale consistency   rnorm(nrow(ind_df), 0, 0.4) )  # Offspring depends on Mass ind_df$Offspring <- rpois(nrow(ind_df), lambda = exp(0.5 + 0.6 * ind_df$Mass))  # Combine into a multiscale list data_list <- list(   year       = year_df,   individual = ind_df )

title: Multiscale Causal Models
text: Pass data named list data frames. names define scale labels uses assign variables. specify structural equations usual: finally can fit model (), specifying multiscale structure variable linking datasets link_vars argument.
code: data_list <- list(   year       = year_df,   individual = ind_df ) equations <- list(   NDVI      ~ Temp,   Mass      ~ NDVI + Temp,   Offspring ~ Mass ) fit <- because(   equations  = equations,   data       = data_list,   family     = c(Offspring = "poisson"),   multiscale = "year > individual",   link_vars  = c(year = "Year"),   n.iter     = 15000,   n.burnin   = 3000 )  summary(fit)

title: Bayesian Missing Data Imputation
text: Missing data ubiquitous ecology evolutionary biology. Traits may measurable individuals, observations may lost, certain variables may systematically unavailable subset observations. missing data handled can profoundly affect quality causal inference. handles missing data full Bayesian imputation: missing values treated unknown parameters estimated simultaneously model parameters MCMC sampling. approach two important advantages common alternatives list-wise deletion multiple imputation (MICE): loss power: observations contribute parameter estimation, even rows missing predictors. Logical consistency: imputation happens inside model, imputed values always respect causal structure (e.g., deterministic relationships variables preserved every MCMC draw). vignette demonstrates use ’s built-imputation extract_imputed() function recover posterior distribution imputed values.

what: Why Bayesian Imputation Within the SEM?
title: Bayesian Missing Data Imputation
text: Consider dataset IsMature defined Age > 2. standard two-step approach (impute first, model) : Impute Age independently using regression variables. Impute IsMature independently based hormone levels body size. Result: marmot aged 6 classified Immature — physically impossible. , IsMature deterministic node defined Age. model always calculates IsMature function imputed Age every MCMC step, guaranteeing logical coherence throughout posterior. See Deterministic Nodes vignette details.

title: Bayesian Missing Data Imputation
text: simulate ecological dataset individual body mass (Mass) affects annual reproductive output (Offspring), mass missing 20% individuals.

title: Bayesian Missing Data Imputation
code: library(because)  set.seed(42) N <- 100  # Predictors (fully observed) Age <- runif(N, 1, 10) Site_Quality <- rnorm(N, 0, 1)  # Body mass (partially observed) Mass <- 5 + 0.8 * Age + 1.2 * Site_Quality + rnorm(N, 0, 1.5)  # Outcome: annual reproductive output Offspring <- rpois(N, lambda = exp(0.3 + 0.15 * Mass))  # Introduce ~20% missingness in Mass miss_idx <- sample(1:N, size = round(0.2 * N)) Mass_obs <- Mass Mass_obs[miss_idx] <- NA  eco_data <- data.frame(   Offspring = Offspring,   Mass = Mass_obs,        # Has NAs   Age = Age,   Site_Quality = Site_Quality )  # How many are missing? cat("Missing Mass values:", sum(is.na(eco_data$Mass)), "\n")

title: Bayesian Missing Data Imputation
text: Missing values data frame automatically detected imputed model fitting. special syntax required — just include column NAs usual: Note: monitor = "" required record posterior distributions imputed values. default monitor = "interpretable", regression coefficients variance components saved, extract_imputed() find imputed nodes.
code: equations <- list(   Mass     ~ Age + Site_Quality,  # Mass depends on Age and Site quality   Offspring ~ Mass                # Offspring depends on Mass (count outcome) )  # Run with monitor = "all" to record imputed values in the posterior fit_imp <- because(   equations = equations,   data      = eco_data,   family    = c(Offspring = "poisson"),   monitor   = "all",     # Essential: saves imputed node posteriors   n.iter    = 15000,   n.burnin  = 3000 )  summary(fit_imp)

title: Bayesian Missing Data Imputation
text: output data frame contains: Variable: variable missing value (Mass). RowIndex: row number original data. Mean, SD: Posterior mean standard deviation imputed value. Q2.5, Q50, Q97.5: 2.5%, 50%, 97.5% credible interval bounds.
code: # Extract posterior summaries for all imputed Mass values imputed <- extract_imputed(fit_imp)  # View the first few rows head(imputed) #   Variable RowIndex     Mean       SD      Q2.5      Q50     Q97.5 # 1     Mass        3   7.234    0.843     5.595    7.218     8.901 # 2     Mass       11   6.119    0.931     4.336    6.098     7.998 # ...

title: Bayesian Missing Data Imputation
text: can plot imputed values credible intervals alongside observed data assess imputation quality: Imputed values (red) fall within plausible range given model — boundary observed values implausible extremes.
code: # Add a column indicating which rows were imputed eco_data$imputed <- is.na(eco_data$Mass)  # Merge imputed summaries back onto the data # (imputed$RowIndex gives row positions in the original data) plot_data <- eco_data plot_data$Mass_mean <- plot_data$Mass for (i in seq_len(nrow(imputed))) {   idx <- imputed$RowIndex[i]   plot_data$Mass_mean[idx] <- imputed$Mean[i] }  library(ggplot2) ggplot(plot_data, aes(x = Age, y = Mass_mean, colour = imputed)) +   geom_point(size = 2, alpha = 0.8) +   scale_colour_manual(     values = c("FALSE" = "steelblue", "TRUE" = "tomato"),     labels = c("Observed", "Imputed")   ) +   labs(     x = "Age", y = "Body Mass",     title = "Observed and Bayesian-Imputed Body Mass",     colour = ""   ) +   theme_minimal()

title: Bayesian Missing Data Imputation
text: can also impute missing values response side. useful want infer likely values unmeasured outcome particular individuals.
code: set.seed(7)  # Some Offspring values are missing (field data collection gaps) eco_data2 <- eco_data eco_data2$Offspring[sample(1:N, 10)] <- NA  fit_imp2 <- because(   equations = list(     Mass     ~ Age + Site_Quality,     Offspring ~ Mass   ),   data    = eco_data2,   family  = c(Offspring = "poisson"),   monitor = "all",   n.iter  = 15000,   n.burnin = 3000,   quiet   = TRUE )  imputed2 <- extract_imputed(fit_imp2) print(imputed2)

what: Example 3: Missing Data and Deterministic Nodes
title: Bayesian Missing Data Imputation
text: mentioned introduction, Bayesian imputation preserves deterministic constraints. demonstrate marmot example Deterministic Nodes vignette:
code: set.seed(99) N <- 80  Age <- runif(N, 0, 8) IsMature <- as.numeric(Age >= 2)  # Deterministic: 1 if age >= 2 Mating_Success <- 2.5 * IsMature + rnorm(N, 0, 0.8)  # Introduce missingness in Age Age_obs <- Age Age_obs[sample(1:N, 15)] <- NA  marmot_data <- data.frame(   Mating_Success = Mating_Success,   Age = Age_obs )  # The formula I(Age >= 2) defines IsMature as a deterministic node. # For every MCMC draw, if Age is imputed as 5, IsMature is automatically set to 1. # There is no inconsistency between Age and IsMature in any posterior sample. fit_marmot <- because(   equations = list(Mating_Success ~ I(Age >= 2)),   data      = marmot_data,   monitor   = "all",   n.iter    = 15000,   n.burnin  = 3000,   quiet     = TRUE )  summary(fit_marmot)

title: Bayesian Missing Data Imputation
text: illustrate cost discarding incomplete observations, fit model using complete cases: imputation model typically produces narrower credible intervals path coefficients uses information, avoids bias non-random missingness patterns.
code: # List-wise deletion complete_data <- eco_data[!is.na(eco_data$Mass), ] cat("Remaining observations:", nrow(complete_data), "of", N, "\n")  fit_listwise <- because(   equations = list(     Mass     ~ Age + Site_Quality,     Offspring ~ Mass   ),   data   = complete_data,   family = c(Offspring = "poisson"),   quiet  = TRUE )  # Compare posterior widths summary(fit_imp)      # Uses all N observations (some imputed) summary(fit_listwise) # Uses only complete cases

title: High-Performance SEM with the NIMBLE Engine

title: High-Performance SEM with the NIMBLE Engine

Since NIMBLE must compile C++ code chain, running parallel allows compilation happen simultaneously different CPU cores, significantly reducing total “wait time” sampling begins.

title: High-Performance SEM with the NIMBLE Engine

High R-hat (>1.05>1.05): parameter fails converge even long burn-many iterations. “Fuzzy” Traceplots: traceplot looks like “snake” long-term trends rather looking like consistent “caterpillar.” Pro-Tip: specific regression coefficient (β\beta) mixing poorly, swapping default RW slice sampler often effective fix.

RW: standard scalar Random Walk Metropolis-Hastings sampler. fast per iteration might require tuning (iterations) reach high effective sample size compared slice sampling. RW_block: multivariate Random Walk sampler. samples block parameters jointly, can faster individual RW samplers parameters strongly correlated. categorical: specialized sampler discrete choice models (e.g., Multinomial Ordinal models). extremely efficient data types.

title: Latent Variables and the MAG Approach
text: causal inference, latent variable variable influences system never measured. Unmeasured common causes particularly problematic create spurious correlations variables affect, can mistaken direct causal effects. standard DAG framework assumes common causes observed. unmeasured common causes, appropriate graphical model Maximal Ancestral Graph (MAG), represents unobserved common causes bidirected edges (e.g., X↔YX \leftrightarrow Y). supports latent variables latent argument, adapts d-separation basis set accordingly using MAG framework Shipley & Douda (2021).

what: Background: DAGs, MAGs, and Bidirected Edges
title: Latent Variables and the MAG Approach
text: standard Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG), every causal relationship represented directed edge (X→YX \Y). variables — observed unobserved — appear nodes. Maximal Ancestral Graph (MAG), latent variables marginalized , leaving observed variables. effect latent common cause LL two observed variables XX YY represented bidirected edge X↔YX \leftrightarrow Y, indicating residuals XX YY correlated. matters d-separation: conditioning different variables different consequences MAG compared DAG, basis set conditional independence claims must derived MAG, original DAG.

title: Latent Variables and the MAG Approach
text: Declare latent variables passing names latent argument (). model must still include equations latent variable (effects observed variables estimated), latent variable present data. treat unobserved node compute MAG-based d-separation basis set automatically.

title: Latent Variables and the MAG Approach
text: monitor 150 individual birds breeding season. measure: Food_Availability (X₁): Territory food resources (observed). Clutch_Size (Y₁): Number eggs laid (observed). Fledgling_Success (Y₂): Number young fledged (observed). hypothesize unmeasured Individual_Quality (L) — combination genetic quality, immune status, experience — affects Clutch_Size Fledgling_Success simultaneously. Without accounting latent variable, correlation Clutch_Size Fledgling_Success might mistakenly attributed direct causal path .

title: Latent Variables and the MAG Approach
text: underlying DAG (latent variable) : marginalise Individual_Quality, MAG contains bidirected edge:
code: Food_Availability → Clutch_Size Food_Availability → Fledgling_Success Individual_Quality → Clutch_Size Individual_Quality → Fledgling_Success Food_Availability → Clutch_Size Food_Availability → Fledgling_Success Clutch_Size ↔ Fledgling_Success   (residual correlation from latent quality)

title: Latent Variables and the MAG Approach
code: library(because)  set.seed(42) N <- 150  # Observed predictor Food_Availability <- rnorm(N, 0, 1)  # Latent variable (unobserved — we simulate it for data generation only) Individual_Quality <- rnorm(N, 0, 1)  # Outcomes driven by both observed and latent causes Clutch_Size <- round(   4 + 0.8 * Food_Availability + 1.2 * Individual_Quality + rnorm(N, 0, 0.5) ) Clutch_Size <- pmax(Clutch_Size, 1)  # Counts must be positive  Fledgling_Success <- round(   2 + 0.6 * Food_Availability + 0.9 * Individual_Quality + rnorm(N, 0, 0.5) ) Fledgling_Success <- pmax(Fledgling_Success, 0)  # Data frame — Individual_Quality is NOT included (it's unobserved) bird_data <- data.frame(   Food_Availability  = scale(Food_Availability)[, 1],   Clutch_Size        = Clutch_Size,   Fledgling_Success  = Fledgling_Success )

what: Fit the Model with a Latent Variable
title: Latent Variables and the MAG Approach
text: summary now includes estimates : beta_Clutch_Size_Food_Availability: effect food clutch size. beta_Fledgling_Success_Food_Availability: effect food fledgling success. beta_Clutch_Size_Individual_Quality: estimated loading latent quality clutch size. beta_Fledgling_Success_Individual_Quality: estimated loading fledgling success. residual correlation Clutch_Size Fledgling_Success captures shared latent influence.
code: # Structural equations: the latent variable appears on the RHS of both equations. # because() detects that Individual_Quality is not in the data and treats it as latent. equations_latent <- list(   Clutch_Size       ~ Food_Availability + Individual_Quality,   Fledgling_Success ~ Food_Availability + Individual_Quality )  fit_latent <- because(   equations = equations_latent,   data      = bird_data,   latent    = "Individual_Quality",  # Declare the latent variable   dsep      = TRUE,                  # Uses MAG-based d-sep basis set   n.iter    = 20000,   n.burnin  = 5000 )  summary(fit_latent)

title: Latent Variables and the MAG Approach
text: latent specified, automatically derives conditional independence basis set MAG rather DAG. MAG-based tests correctly account bidirected edge Clutch_Size Fledgling_Success. Without latent variable, DAG incorrectly imply Clutch_Size ⊥ Fledgling_Success | {Food_Availability} — test fail data shared latent influence. MAG representation, test dropped basis set (bidirected edges imply residual dependence even conditioning observed parents), giving honest assessment model fit.
code: # Inspect d-separation results (MAG-based basis set) # The tests shown reflect the independence claims implied by the MAG, # not the original full DAG. print(fit_latent$dsep_results)

title: Latent Variables and the MAG Approach
text: latent_method argument controls latent variable integrated model: "correlations" (default): Introduces residual correlation parameter observed descendants latent variable. Equivalent marginalising latent estimating free covariance. "factor": Models latent variable explicit factor, estimating loadings precision. interpretable requires identifiability constraints (see fix_latent).
code: fit_factor <- because(   equations  = equations_latent,   data       = bird_data,   latent     = "Individual_Quality",   latent_method = "factor",       # Explicit factor model   fix_latent    = "loading",      # Fix first loading to 1 for identifiability   standardize_latent = TRUE,      # Standardise the latent factor (SD = 1)   n.iter     = 20000,   n.burnin   = 5000 )  summary(fit_factor)

title: Latent Variables and the MAG Approach
text: using latent_method = "factor", factor scale identified without constraint. fix_latent = "loading" fixes first loading (first equation’s latent effect) 1, anchoring latent scale.

title: Latent Variables and the MAG Approach
text: Setting standardize_latent = TRUE standardises latent variable unit variance, making loadings comparable across variables easier interpret standardised path coefficients.

what: Comparing Models: With and Without the Latent Variable
title: Latent Variables and the MAG Approach

title: Creating Custom Distribution Families
text: natively supports common distribution families (Gaussian, Poisson, Binomial, Negative Binomial, Ordinal, etc.). specialised data types, because_family() function lets define JAGS-compatible distribution drop-replacement need modify core package. need custom family? Robust regression: Student-t data heavy tails outliers. Bounded proportions: Beta distribution continuous proportions (0, 1) (e.g., percentage cover, survival rates). Truncated distributions: Modelling quantities physically bounded (e.g., speeds negative, body temperatures within viable range). Specialised ecological distributions: Log-normal, Gamma, compound distributions yet natively supported.

title: Creating Custom Distribution Families
code: because_family(   name,           # A short name (e.g., "student_t", "beta_prop")   jags_likelihood,# JAGS likelihood code with placeholders   link    = "identity",  # "identity", "log", or "logit"   extra_priors = NULL,   # Additional JAGS prior statements   description  = NULL    # Optional human-readable description )

title: Creating Custom Distribution Families
text: jags_likelihood string uses four placeholders replaces model-build time: extra_priors uses placeholders appended outside likelihood loop — use additional parameters like degrees freedom dispersion.

title: Creating Custom Distribution Families
text: Student-t distribution ν\nu degrees freedom heavier tails Gaussian, making robust outliers. JAGS provides dt(mu, tau, nu). because_family() returns constructor function (student_t). Call arguments create family object pass ():
code: library(because)  student_t <- because_family(   name = "student_t",    # JAGS dt(mu, tau, nu): location, precision, degrees of freedom   jags_likelihood = "{response}[{i}] ~ dt({mu}[{i}], {tau}, df_{response})",    link = "identity",    # Prior on degrees of freedom: wide uniform between 2 (very heavy) and 100 (near-Gaussian)   extra_priors = c("df_{response} ~ dunif(2, 100)"),    description = "Student-t for robust regression with estimated degrees of freedom" ) set.seed(42) N <- 80  X <- rnorm(N)  # Heavy-tailed response: many mild values plus a few large outliers Y <- 1.5 * X + rt(N, df = 3)  robust_data <- data.frame(Y = Y, X = X)  # Gaussian model (potentially biased by outliers) fit_gaussian <- because(   equations = list(Y ~ X),   data      = robust_data,   quiet     = TRUE )  # Student-t model (robust to outliers) fit_robust <- because(   equations = list(Y ~ X),   data      = robust_data,   family    = c(Y = student_t()),   # Pass the constructor result   quiet     = TRUE )  summary(fit_robust) # df_Y: estimated degrees of freedom — small values (2–5) indicate heavy tails

title: Creating Custom Distribution Families
text: genuinely heavy-tailed data, Student-t model typically lower WAIC Gaussian.
code: fit_gaussian_waic <- because(   equations = list(Y ~ X), data = robust_data, WAIC = TRUE, quiet = TRUE ) fit_robust_waic <- because(   equations = list(Y ~ X), data = robust_data,   family = c(Y = student_t()), WAIC = TRUE, quiet = TRUE )  because_compare(fit_gaussian_waic, fit_robust_waic)

what: Example 2: Beta Family for Proportions in (0, 1)
title: Creating Custom Distribution Families
text: Continuous proportions — percentage vegetation cover, task completion rates, dietary proportions — bounded 0 1. Gaussian model can predict values outside range; Beta distribution natural choice. Beta distribution parameterised mean μ∈(0,1)\mu \(0,1) precision (concentration) parameter ϕ>0\phi > 0: Y∼Beta(μϕ,(1−μ)ϕ)Y \sim \text{Beta}(\mu \phi, (1-\mu)\phi) parameterisation, variance μ(1−μ)/(ϕ+1)\mu(1-\mu)/(\phi + 1): larger ϕ\phi means less variance around μ\mu.
code: beta_prop <- because_family(   name = "beta_prop",    # JAGS: dbeta(shape1, shape2)   # shape1 = mu * phi, shape2 = (1 - mu) * phi   jags_likelihood = paste0(     "{response}[{i}] ~ dbeta(",     "{mu}[{i}] * phi_{response}, ",     "(1 - {mu}[{i}]) * phi_{response})"   ),    link = "logit",   # Logit link maps (-Inf, Inf) to (0, 1)    # Prior on the precision (concentration) parameter   extra_priors = c("phi_{response} ~ dgamma(1, 0.1)"),    description = "Beta distribution for proportions in (0, 1) with logit link" ) set.seed(7) N <- 100  # Predictor: grazing intensity (standardised) Grazing <- rnorm(N, 0, 1)  # Response: vegetation cover proportion in (0, 1) # True relationship on the logit scale logit_mu <- -0.5 - 0.8 * Grazing mu_true  <- 1 / (1 + exp(-logit_mu)) Cover    <- rbeta(N, shape1 = mu_true * 5, shape2 = (1 - mu_true) * 5)  prop_data <- data.frame(Cover = Cover, Grazing = Grazing)  fit_beta <- because(   equations = list(Cover ~ Grazing),   data      = prop_data,   family    = c(Cover = beta_prop()),   n.iter    = 15000,   n.burnin  = 3000 )  summary(fit_beta) # beta_Cover_Grazing: effect on the logit scale (negative = grazing reduces cover) # phi_Cover: estimated precision (higher = lower variance around predicted mean) # Convert from logit scale to probability scale using marginal effects me_beta <- marginal_effects(fit_beta) print(me_beta) # Effect: expected change in Cover (proportion units) per 1-unit increase in Grazing

title: Creating Custom Distribution Families
text: biological quantities strictly positive: body mass, flight speed, enzyme concentration. standard Gaussian model can assign positive probability negative values. JAGS supports truncation notation T(lower, upper).
code: pos_gaussian <- because_family(   name = "pos_gaussian",   jags_likelihood = "{response}[{i}] ~ dnorm({mu}[{i}], {tau}) T(0, )",   link = "log",   # Log link ensures the linear predictor maps to positive means   description     = "Truncated Gaussian (positive support only)" )  set.seed(99) N <- 100 Mass <- rnorm(N, mean = 50, sd = 10) Speed <- exp(1.2 + 0.05 * Mass + rnorm(N, 0, 0.3))  # Strictly positive  pos_data <- data.frame(Speed = Speed, Mass = scale(Mass)[, 1])  fit_pos <- because(   equations = list(Speed ~ Mass),   data      = pos_data,   family    = c(Speed = pos_gaussian()),   quiet     = TRUE )  summary(fit_pos)

Note logo: package hexagon sticker features mathematical symbol “”, represented three dots inverted triangle (∵\).

title: Bayesian Estimation of Causal Effects
text: simplifies process running complex Bayesian Structural Equation Models automatically generating JAGS NIMBLE code standard R formulas. Key features include: Dual-Engine Support: Choose simplicity JAGS high-performance C++ backend NIMBLE. Automatic Model Generation: Builds complex BUGS/NIMBLE models directly list structural equations. Generalized Covariance Structures: Supports specification custom covariance structures data. Missing Data Support: Imputes missing values response predictor variables (assuming data missing random (MAR) completely random (MCAR). Measurement Error: Accounts measurement error providing repeated measures known error variances. Distribution families: Supports Gaussian, Binomial, Multinomial, Ordinal, Poisson,Negative Binomial well Zero Inflated Poisson (ZIP) Zero Inflated Negative Binomial (ZINP) distributions. Categorical Predictors: Automatic handling factor variables dummy variable expansion. Polynomial Interaction Terms: Easily include polynomial terms interactions structural equations. Hierachical Models: Support random effects multi-level structures. Latent Variables: Support explicitly including Latent variables modelling induced correlations latent common causes Maximum Acyclic Graph (MAG) method. Causal inference tools: Native support d-separation m-separation testing, mediation analysis, unified marginal effects comparing cross-family coefficients. Marginal Effects: New marginal_effects(fit) function converts log-odds/latent scales “Expected Change” units (0-1 probabilities unit values) easy comparison across different variable types. Parallel Computing: Run MCMC chains parallel multi-core systems faster computation. Visualisation tools: Functions visualizing model structures posterior distributions. Extension packages: Full S3 support extension packages build core functionality.

title: Run a Bayesian Structural Equation Model (Because) — because
text: equations list R formulas describing structural model. formula represents causal path (e.g., Y ~ X1 + X2). Categorical predictors automatically handled. data data frame containing variables. hierarchical models, must named list data frames (one per level). id_col Character string column identifying units (e.g. species names). structure Optional covariance structure (e.g., phylogenetic tree spatial matrix) modeling correlated residuals. engine Bayesian backend: "jags" (default) "nimble". n.chains Number independent MCMC chains (default = 3). n.iter Total MCMC iterations per chain (default = 12500). n.burnin Number burn-iterations (default = 20% n.iter). dsep Logical; TRUE, performs d-separation tests evaluate global fit DAG data. Highly recommended causal validation. family named character vector specifying distribution response (e.g., c(Y = "poisson", M = "gaussian")). Supports "gaussian" (default), "poisson", "binomial", "gamma", "lognormal", "bernoulli", "ordinal", "occupancy". latent Optional character vector latent (unmeasured) variables. parallel Logical; TRUE, runs MCMC chains parallel. n.cores Number CPU cores parallel execution. random Optional formula global random intercepts (e.g., ~(1|Site)). Deprecated favor inline specification: Y ~ X + (1|Site). levels (Hierarchical) Optional named list mapping variables home levels. omitted, attempt auto-detect levels based column availability. hierarchy (Hierarchical) string defining nesting structure (e.g., "site > individual"). Semicolons separate parallel branches (e.g., "site > obs; species > obs"). link_vars (Hierarchical) named character vector specifying columns used link data frames across levels (e.g., c(site = "SiteID")). ... Additional arguments passed underlying model engines.

title: Run a Bayesian Structural Equation Model (Because) — because
text: object class "" containing: samples MCMC samples (mcmc.list). parameter_map Data frame mapping parameter names model variables. model JAGS model code. summary Summary posterior samples. dsep_results List d-separation test results (dsep=TRUE). DIC Deviance Information Criterion (DIC=TRUE). WAIC Watanabe-Akaike Information Criterion (WAIC=TRUE).

title: Run a Bayesian Structural Equation Model (Because) — because
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # 1. Simple Path Model df <- data.frame(Y = rnorm(100), M = rnorm(100), X = rnorm(100)) eqs <- list(M ~ X, Y ~ M + X) fit <- because(eqs, data = df, dsep = TRUE) summary(fit)  # 2. Hierarchical Model (Auto-detection) # Suppose we have year-level data and individual-level data year_df <- data.frame(Year = 1:5, Temp = rnorm(5)) ind_df  <- data.frame(Year = rep(1:5, each=10), Mass = rnorm(50)) data_list <- list(yr = year_df, ind = ind_df)  # Mass depends on Temp (cross-level link via Year) fit_h <- because(   equations = list(Mass ~ Temp),   data      = data_list,   hierarchy = "yr > ind",   link_vars = c(yr = "Year") )  # 3. Random Intercepts (lme4-style) # Note: Grouping variables (Site) must be in the data df$Site <- rep(1:10, each=10) fit_re <- because(list(Y ~ X + (1|Site)), data = df) } # }

title: Extract d-separation statements from a structural equation model — because_dsep
text: function implements basis set approach d-separation testing (Shipley 2000, 2009, 2016). standard DAGs without latent variables, identifies pairs non-adjacent variables creates conditional independence tests. latent variables specified, function uses DAG--MAG conversion (Shipley & Douma 2021) identify m-separation statements induced correlations among observed variables arise shared latent common causes. Deterministic nodes (interaction terms :B, arithmetic transformations (^2)) kept explicit intermediate nodes DAG, following D-separation extension Geiger, Verma & Pearl (1990).  ensures basis set includes independence tests condition deterministic term (e.g. \(TL \perp BM \mid \{BM{:}M\}\)), silently dropped interaction collapsed component variables.

title: Generate a JAGS model string for Bayesian SEM (Because) — because_model
text: equations list model formulas. is_multi_structure Logical (Internal). TRUE, handles 3D Precision arrays. latent_method Method handling latent variables ("correlations" "explicit"). structures named list structural objects (e.g. matrices, trees) include correlations. random_structure_names Optional character vector structural names applied variables. random_terms Optional list random effects (response, group). vars_with_na Optional character vector response variable names missing data. variables use element-wise likelihoods instead multivariate normal. induced_correlations Optional list variable pairs induced correlations latent variables. element character vector length 2 specifying pair variables share latent common cause. variability Optional character vector named character vector variable names measurement error within-species variability. named, names variable names values type variability: "se" (mean standard error) "reps" (repeated measures). unnamed, defaults "se" specified variables. "se": Expects Var_mean Var_se data. model fixes observation error: Var_mean ~ dnorm(Var, 1/Var_se^2). "reps": Expects Var_obs (matrix) N_reps_Var (vector) data. model estimates observation error: Var_obs[,j] ~ dnorm(Var[], Var_tau). family Optional named character vector specifying family/distribution response variables. Default "gaussian" variables. Supported values: "gaussian", "binomial", "multinomial". "binomial" variables, model uses logit link Bernoulli likelihood, phylogenetic correlation modeled latent scale. standardize_latent Logical (default TRUE). TRUE, standardizes latent variables unit variance. poly_terms (Internal) List polynomial terms model generation. latent Optional character vector latent variable names. categorical_vars Optional character vector categorical variable names. fix_latent Variable fix latent identification. fix_residual_variance Optional numeric value named vector fix residual variance. priors Optional named list custom priors. hierarchical_info Optional list containing data hierarchy (levels, link_vars). engine Bayesian engine use ("jags" "nimble"). quiet Logical; suppress status messages.

code: eqs <- list(BR ~ BM, S ~ BR, G ~ BR, L ~ BR) cat(because_model(eqs, is_multi_structure = TRUE)$model) #> model { #>   # Common structures and priors #>   # Structural equations #>  #>   for (i in 1:N) { #>     mu_BR[i] <- alpha_BR + beta_BR_BM*BM[i] #>   } #>   for (i in 1:N) { #>     mu_S[i] <- alpha_S + beta_S_BR*BR[i] #>   } #>   for (i in 1:N) { #>     mu_G[i] <- alpha_G + beta_G_BR*BR[i] #>   } #>   for (i in 1:N) { #>     mu_L[i] <- alpha_L + beta_L_BR*BR[i] #>   } #>   # Multivariate normal likelihoods #>   for (i in 1:N) { #>     BR[i] ~ dnorm(mu_BR[i], tau_res_BR) #>     log_lik_BR[i] <- logdensity.norm(BR[i], mu_BR[i], tau_res_BR) #>   } #>   for (i in 1:N) { #>     S[i] ~ dnorm(mu_S[i], tau_res_S) #>     log_lik_S[i] <- logdensity.norm(S[i], mu_S[i], tau_res_S) #>   } #>   for (i in 1:N) { #>     G[i] ~ dnorm(mu_G[i], tau_res_G) #>     log_lik_G[i] <- logdensity.norm(G[i], mu_G[i], tau_res_G) #>   } #>   for (i in 1:N) { #>     L[i] ~ dnorm(mu_L[i], tau_res_L) #>     log_lik_L[i] <- logdensity.norm(L[i], mu_L[i], tau_res_L) #>   } #>   # Priors for structural parameters #>   alpha_BR ~ dnorm(0, 0.01) #>   sigma_BR_res ~ dunif(0, 100) #>   tau_res_BR <- 1 / (sigma_BR_res * sigma_BR_res) #>   alpha_S ~ dnorm(0, 0.01) #>   sigma_S_res ~ dunif(0, 100) #>   tau_res_S <- 1 / (sigma_S_res * sigma_S_res) #>   alpha_G ~ dnorm(0, 0.01) #>   sigma_G_res ~ dunif(0, 100) #>   tau_res_G <- 1 / (sigma_G_res * sigma_G_res) #>   alpha_L ~ dnorm(0, 0.01) #>   sigma_L_res ~ dunif(0, 100) #>   tau_res_L <- 1 / (sigma_L_res * sigma_L_res) #>   beta_BR_BM ~ dnorm(0, 0.01) #>   beta_S_BR ~ dnorm(0, 0.01) #>   beta_G_BR ~ dnorm(0, 0.01) #>   beta_L_BR ~ dnorm(0, 0.01) #>   # Phylogenetic uncertainty weighting #>   for (k in 1:Ntree) { #>     p_tree[k] <- 1/Ntree #>   } #>   K ~ dcat(p_tree[1:Ntree]) #> }

title: Calculate WAIC with Standard Errors for a Because Model — because_waic
text: function automatically called WAIC = TRUE. can also called manually. model originally fitted WAIC = TRUE (pointwise log-likelihoods missing), function automatically refit model (using short MCMC run) compute .

title: Calculate WAIC with Standard Errors for a Because Model — because_waic
text: Widely Applicable Information Criterion (WAIC) calculated : $$WAIC = -2 \times (lppd - p_{waic})$$ : \(lppd = \sum_{=1}^N \log(\frac{1}{S} \sum_{s=1}^S \exp(log\_lik_{}))\) log pointwise predictive density \(p_{waic} = \sum_{=1}^N \text{var}(log\_lik_{})\) effective number parameters WAIC Algorithm: lpd (log pointwise predictive density): observation \(\), compute \(\log(\text{mean}(\exp(\text{log\_lik}_i)))\) across MCMC samples p_waic: observation \(\), compute \(\text{var}(\text{log\_lik}_i)\) across MCMC samples elpd_waic: \(\text{lpd}_i - \text{p\_waic}_i\) observation waic: \(-2 \times \sum \text{elpd\_waic}_i\)

title: Calculate WAIC with Standard Errors for a Because Model — because_waic
text: Standard errors WAIC calculated using pointwise contributions: $$SE(WAIC) = \sqrt{N \times \text{var}(waic_i)}$$ \(waic_i = -2 \times (lppd_i - p_{waic,})\).

title: Calculate WAIC with Standard Errors for a Because Model — because_waic
text: Vehtari, ., Gelman, ., & Gabry, J. (2017). Practical Bayesian model evaluation using leave-one-cross-validation WAIC. Statistics Computing, 27(5), 1413-1432.

title: Calculate WAIC with Standard Errors for a Because Model — because_waic
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{   # Fit model with WAIC monitoring   fit <- because(data, tree, equations, WAIC = TRUE)    # View WAIC with standard errors   fit$WAIC   #             Estimate   SE   # elpd_waic   -617.3   12.4   # p_waic        12.3    3.1   # waic        1234.5   24.8    # Compare two models   fit1$WAIC   fit2$WAIC   # Model with lower WAIC is preferred   # Difference is significant if |WAIC1 - WAIC2| > 2 * sqrt(SE1^2 + SE2^2) } # }

"sugiyama" — hierarchical/layered layout; useful simple chains. "fr" — Fruchterman-Reingold spring layout. "nicely" — automatic choice based graph properties. "circle" — nodes arranged circle. Override node positions entirely coords argument. latent Character vector latent variable names. Overrides model's latent variables provided. NULL (default) plotting formulas, latent variables automatically detected follow SEM naming convention (e.g., L1, L2, Latent1, lat_foo) appear RHS equations. node_size Size nodes (default 14). node_color Color node border (default "black"). node_fill Color node interior (default "white"). node_stroke Thickness node border (default 1.5). text_size Size node labels (default 3.5). edge_label_size Size edge coefficient labels (default 3). Reduce crowded plots (e.g., 2 2.5). edge_width_range Vector length 2 defining range arrow widths (min, max) based effect size. edge_color_scheme Character; one "directional" (default), "binary", "monochrome". "directional" colors edges red/blue/grey based effect direction whether 95\ "binary" colors edges black/grey based whether 95\ "monochrome" colors edges black. show_coefficients Logical; whether print coefficient values edges (fitted models). coords Optional named list coordinates nodes, e.g. list(= c(1, 1), B = c(2, 2)). provided, override layout algorithm. Partial coordinates supported: nodes included coords positioned according automatic layout. deterministic nodes (interactions powers), can use original formula string key (e.g., "(age^2)" = c(x, y) "X:Y" = c(x, y)). family Optional named character vector families response variables. type Character; either "raw" (default) "marginal" effects. multinomial_probabilities Logical; TRUE, expands multinomial categories.

text: Interaction Deterministic Nodes: Interaction terms (e.g. BM:M) () transformations rendered explicit intermediate nodes (grey diamonds), following Interaction DAG (IDAG) convention Attia, Holliday & Oldmeadow (2022). makes deterministic nature terms visually clear consistent because_dsep treats d-separation. Latent Variable Auto-detection: plotting list formulas, plot_dag automatically identifies latent variables (rendering circles) match common SEM naming conventions (like L1, Latent, lat_climate) never appear response (LHS) equation. Manual Positioning Formula Strings: using coords, can specify positions interaction power nodes using formula representation list key. example: coords = list(weight = c(0,0), "(age^2)" = c(1,1), "sex:age" = c(2,2)). node specified list maintain position automatic layout. Random Effects: Formula terms containing random effects (e.g., (1|year)) automatically filtered structural DAG visualization.
